activities_projects.title: The Economic Chain Reaction
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the structural causes of the empty treasury and the subsequent tax crisis
steps: Identify the specific reasons why the French treasury was empty in 1774[cite: 62, 63, 64].
Calculate the total debt in livres after the American war of independence and the interest burden placed on the state[cite: 66, 67, 68].
Diagram the sequence of events from 'Bad Harvest' to 'Subsistence Crisis' using Fig. 4 as a guide[cite: 134, 138, 153].
Explain why increasing taxes alone would not have sufficed under the existing estate system[cite: 70, 83].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Connects macro-economic failure to social unrest and political desperation
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of financial data and logical flow of the economic flowchart (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Philosophical Roots of Revolution
activities_projects.objective: Compare the political theories that challenged the absolute monarchy
steps: Research the core arguments in Locke’s 'Two Treatises of Government' and Rousseau’s 'The Social Contract'[cite: 149, 150, 156].
Summarize Montesquieu’s proposal in 'The Spirit of the Laws' regarding the division of power[cite: 157].
Identify how the American Constitution served as a practical example for French thinkers[cite: 158, 159].
Discuss how these ideas were spread among the illiterate population[cite: 160, 161].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes how intellectual movements provide the framework for political change
activities_projects.evaluation: Depth of comparison between the three philosophers (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Estates General Deadlock
activities_projects.objective: Understand the procedural conflict that triggered the National Assembly's formation
steps: Contrast the traditional voting principle of the Estates General with the Third Estate's demand for 'one member, one vote'[cite: 198, 200].
Describe the seating arrangement at Versailles and how it physically represented social inequality[cite: 194].
Draft a 'protest letter' from the perspective of a Third Estate representative explaining why they walked out on 5 May 1789[cite: 192, 202].
Explain the significance of the Tennis Court Oath in limiting royal power[cite: 204, 205].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies the shift from a feudal assembly to a sovereign national body
activities_projects.evaluation: Argumentative strength of the protest letter and role-play (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Women’s Political Activism: Olympe de Gouges
activities_projects.objective: Critically evaluate the gender-based contradictions in revolutionary rights
steps: Compare the 'Declaration of the Rights of Man and Citizen' with Olympe de Gouges' 'Declaration of the Rights of Woman and Citizen'[cite: 345, 555, 564].
Use a Venn Diagram to show which rights were shared and which were unique to De Gouges’ manifesto[cite: 565, 566, 567].
Analyze the arguments used by Chaumette to justify the closure of women's clubs[cite: 581, 582, 591].
Formulate a rebuttal to Chaumette from the perspective of a member of the Society of Revolutionary and Republican Women[cite: 524, 580].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Critically assesses the concept of 'universal rights' during the Revolution
activities_projects.evaluation: Critical thinking in the rebuttal and accuracy of the Venn diagram (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Triangular Slave Trade and its Paradox
activities_projects.objective: Analyze the conflict between revolutionary ideals and economic interests
steps: Map the 'Triangular Slave Trade' routes involving Europe, Africa, and the Americas[cite: 598, 599].
Identify the specific commodities that made the slave trade essential to port cities like Bordeaux and Nantes[cite: 596, 602].
Explain why the National Assembly hesitated to abolish slavery despite the Declaration of Rights[cite: 604, 605].
Contrast the Convention’s 1794 decree with Napoleon’s 1804 policy on slavery[cite: 606, 607].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the global economic context and the contradictions of the Revolution
activities_projects.evaluation: Clarity of the map and explanation of the legislative delays (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Constitutional Monarchy vs. Republic
activities_projects.objective: Differentiate between the political systems of 1791 and 1792
steps: Using Fig. 7, explain how the 1791 system of 'indirect election' worked[cite: 289, 307, 309, 310].
Define the powers of 'Veto' and 'Control' held by the King and the Assembly[cite: 293, 296, 299].
Describe the events of 10 August 1792 that led to the imprisonment of the royal family[cite: 447, 448].
List the changes in voting rights when France transitioned to a Republic under the Convention[cite: 449, 450].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between different stages of revolutionary governance
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct explanation of political terminology and transitions (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Logic of the Terror
activities_projects.objective: Examine the conflicting definitions of 'Liberty' during 1793-1794
steps: Analyze Robespierre's speech: Why did he believe 'terror' was necessary for 'democracy'?[cite: 487, 488].
Contrast this with Desmoulins' view of liberty as 'Happiness, Reason, Equality, Justice'[cite: 482, 483].
List the specific economic measures (price ceilings, rationing) that characterized the Reign of Terror[cite: 464, 465, 466].
Explain the eventual downfall of Robespierre and the rise of the Directory[cite: 470, 471, 500].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Evaluates how extreme political circumstances can lead to the suspension of civil liberties
activities_projects.evaluation: Balanced analysis of the two conflicting viewpoints in Source D (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Abolition of Censorship and the Public Sphere
activities_projects.objective: Evaluate the impact of freedom of the press on everyday life
steps: Explain the 'Abolition of Censorship' law passed after 1789[cite: 631, 632].
Discuss how the Declaration of Rights made 'freedom of speech' a natural right[cite: 633].
Create a mock front page for a 1790 French newspaper reporting on either the fall of the Bastille or the abolition of feudal dues[cite: 236, 242, 634].
Describe how plays and songs were used to convey philosophical ideas to the masses[cite: 638, 639].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands the role of media in shaping public opinion and revolutionary culture
activities_projects.evaluation: Historical accuracy and creative use of 'Source' material in the newspaper (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Napoleon as a Modernizer
activities_projects.objective: Assess the administrative legacy of the Napoleonic era
steps: Identify the administrative reforms introduced by Napoleon (e.g., Civil Code, decimal system)[cite: 651].
Explain the shift in perception of Napoleonic armies from 'liberators' to 'invaders'[cite: 652, 653].
Evaluate the long-term impact of his reforms on Europe even after his defeat at Waterloo[cite: 654].
Research the 'Napoleonic Code' and list three ways it protected private property[cite: 651].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Synthesizes the role of a military dictator in spreading revolutionary administrative ideals
activities_projects.evaluation: Comprehensive list of reforms and their long-term significance (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Global Legacy: India and Beyond
activities_projects.objective: Trace the influence of the French Revolution on anti-colonial movements
steps: Identify the two Indian individuals mentioned in the text who responded to French revolutionary ideas[cite: 664].
Summarize Raja Rammohan Roy’s reaction to the July Revolution of 1830[cite: 668, 669].
Explain how colonised peoples 'reworked' the idea of freedom into sovereign nation-state movements[cite: 663].
List three democratic rights we enjoy today in India that have roots in the French Revolution[cite: 684].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Connects European history to global and national political developments
activities_projects.evaluation: Ability to link 18th-century French ideas to modern Indian democracy (10 marks)
